title: Freezing Early Layers
concept: Freezing early layers keeps general feature detectors such as edges, corners, and textures fixed, so a pretrained model can reuse broad visual knowledge while a new task learns on top of it.

exercises.prompt: Recap: In transfer learning, what valuable thing does a pretrained network give you at the start?

options.text: Guaranteed perfect accuracy on the new task
A dataset with labels automatically included

exercises.explain: Exactly. Freezing decides which of those learned weights should stay unchanged.

cards.title: Keep the foundation, remodel the top
cards.body: When a building has a solid foundation, you usually keep it and remodel the rooms above it. Early neural layers are often like that foundation: they already detect broad patterns that many tasks still need.

cards.title: What it means to freeze a layer
cards.body: To freeze a layer means its weights are kept fixed during the new training run. Backpropagation still flows through the network, but the frozen weights do not update. In transfer learning, engineers often freeze early layers because they tend to contain general features that remain useful.

cards.title: Do not recalibrate the microscope lenses
cards.body: If a microscope already has well-calibrated lenses, you do not loosen them every time you study a new specimen. You keep the optics stable and change the part of the workflow that interprets what you see. Freezing early layers follows that logic.

cards.title: Flower classifier with a frozen visual backbone
cards.body: Imagine a model pretrained on a huge photo dataset. For a small flower-species project, the early convolutional layers may already know edges, petals, textures, and color transitions. Freezing those layers lets the model keep that general visual knowledge while training a new classifier on top.

cards.title: Frozen early layers do not mean the whole model stops learning
cards.body: A common myth is that freezing some layers makes transfer learning pointless. Not true. The upper layers or new output head can still learn the new task. Freezing simply says, “these basic detectors are already good enough; leave them alone for now.”

cards.title: Frozen base vs. editable top
cards.body: Different parts of the network can play different roles during transfer learning.

left.text:
Keep edges/textures/corners fixed
Preserve broad learned patterns
Fewer weights change

right.text:
Adapt to the new labels
Learn task-specific combinations
Handle the new decision boundary

exercises.prompt: Why are early layers often the first ones engineers freeze in vision transfer learning?

options.text: They often learn general low-level patterns useful across many image tasks
They contain the class names for the new dataset
They are too small to participate in backpropagation
They can only be used once in a model’s lifetime

exercises.explain: Correct. Early layers usually capture broad visual ingredients that are reusable.

exercises.prompt: True or false: If you freeze early layers, no part of the network can learn the new task.
exercises.explain: False. The unfrozen layers, especially the top of the network, can still adapt to the new task.

exercises.sentence: When a layer is frozen, its weights stay __ during the new training run.
exercises.explain: Exactly. Frozen weights are held fixed rather than updated.

exercises.prompt: Which setup best matches the idea of freezing early layers?

options.text: Keep the first visual blocks unchanged while training a new classifier head for product photos
Reset the earliest filters to random values every epoch
Delete the early layers so the model never sees edges or textures

exercises.explain: Right. Freezing preserves the broad visual detectors while later parts specialize.

exercises.prompt: You have a small labeled dataset and limited compute. What is the main reason to freeze early layers in a pretrained vision model?

options.text: Those layers already capture general visual patterns, so keeping them fixed can save training effort and reduce unnecessary change
Frozen layers automatically generate new labels for the dataset
Freezing turns a classifier into an unsupervised model
Early layers are always wrong for all new tasks, so they should be ignored

exercises.explain: Exactly. Freezing early layers keeps the reusable base and lets you focus learning where adaptation matters most.
